reason: Both answers correctly identify the core judo (change the axis, target segments where the incumbent's breadth is a liability, get ahead of the RFP/eval criteria, qualify out of pure checklist bake-offs) and both are actionable and well-organized. Answer B, however, opens with a "Sketch" section that reads like exposed scratch notes/thinking rather than part of the actual answer — it's redundant with the answer that follows and adds clutter without adding value (plus an unexplained name-drop of April Dunford that isn't unpacked). Answer A is equally comprehensive (arguably a shade more concrete, e.g. "give champions your own evaluation rubric," "publish it, demo it live") and is delivered without the throat-clearing, making it tighter and more directly useful to a busy reader.
